sentences.text: Well, there was one man in our group, in our barrack, [INAUDIBLE], in our sleeping quarters, who was a tremendously optimistic person.
Besides that, he worked in Schindler's office.
And, from time to time, he heard news on the radio.
But besides hearing the news, he wanted to keep us alive.
So he used to come every evening and tell us all kind of the stories about victories of the Allies, how the Germans are on the run, and how the Russians are getting close.
And like a hundred people used to surround him and listen to his stories.
And he had some-- some were factual, and some were just improvised in order to-- to keep our spirits up.
But as the war was coming to an end, he listened to the radio, and he knew already that the Germans are in Prague, and that they're going ahead.
And we were in eastern part of Czechoslovakia, in Sudetenland.
So we know that the end is coming.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As I told you, at one point, news came that the Russian POWs were digging ditches.

And the Czech underground, which was always in touch with us, informed us that the Russian POWs are digging ditches for us-- that Leopold, the commandant of the camp, wants to kill everybody in the camp.
Schindler found out about it, and he went to Berlin.
How he did it, I don't know, but he-- a letter was sent from Leopold's unit that he should go right away to Prague and report to an SS unit in Prague.
And actually, he was sent to Prague when Prague was already surrounded by the Russians.
And it was all Schindler's doing, that he got rid of him.
Otherwise, he was afraid-- he never knew what Leopold could do.
At that point when Leopold left-- and we were left already, by that time, with older men who were the guards, because all the younger men were sent to the front.
So Schindler felt quite secure.
A few days before the end of the war, the underground came out-- and I was part of it.
And we told Schindler like that, 'You tell the guards that if they leave all the weapons, and they leave, we'll let them go.
If not, the Czech underground and we are going to fight them, and none of them will come out alive.' And Schindler proposed it to the guards, and the guards were more than happy to leave.
They were all older family men, and-- and they knew the war is coming to an end.
They got on trucks and they left.
And we were without the guards.
And we took over the camp-- the underground.
I got a red band on my arm, and I had a pistol.
And I didn't know what to do with it, but I had a pistol.
And at that point, Churchill had the victory speech in England, and Schindler put it on a loudspeaker system in the factory.

Being that I had English lessons before the war for several years-- not that I was that good-- but I was taking lessons all the time in English-- I could understand, hearing the-- what Churchill was saying, and translating it to my friends around me.
And while I was stood there, I just couldn't believe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that here I am.
The Germans still occupy this part of Czechoslovakia.
And I am listening to-- to Churchill.
When it dawned on me, it was something that my imagination never-- could never form a-- a moment like that.
And this is another thing that I'll never forget.
I never forget I can hear Churchill speaking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that point, we took over the camp.
And for four days, we were without the Germans and without the Russians.
But we decided that when the Russians will come and they'll get hold of Schindler, Schindler is a dead man.
So we decided that Schindler has to leave.
And we gave him an escort of six people-- five men and a woman-- who went with him to protect him.
And he left with those six people in his car and a truck behind.
With his wife.
With his wife.
And we were there.
And the Russians came in.
When the moment the Russians came in, I can tell you, the liberation was not a happy one.
They walked into the camp, and the Russian officer said, you are free people.
You don't have to be afraid anymore.
The Germans are defeated.
Just go out and have a good time.
And there was a Jewish soldier among the Russians who was from Vilna, which was part of Poland.
And he said in Yiddish, 'Don't believe what he says.

You're not free people, because they are as bad as-- as the Nazis.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a Russian officer who-- who came into the camp and said, 'Where are you people going to go?' He said, 'They don't like you in the East, and they don't like you in the West.'
Well, we decided that we will go back to Krakow looking for relatives.
My father had brothers and a sister.
And I had cousins.
And so we started on the journey back home.
The journey back home, we were like seven people.
Among them was Rabbi Lewertow, who later was chief rabbi of Krakow.
We actually walked back to Krakow from-- from Sudeten.
The railroads were destroyed.
And we started walking.
Wherever there was a stretch of a railroad for 10 miles or 50 miles that was working, we used to get on the trains.
And we always had preference.
The Czechs always gave us preference.
They threw out everybody else from the car, and they gave us the seats.
They were unbelievable.
We came to one city called [?
Olomuniec. ?] And the mayor, who was appointed by, the new Communist mayor, came out and he greeted us, and he told his aides to take us to the best hotel.
They took us to the best hotel.
They brought us food.
Because otherwise we used to sleep outside, and we used to-- to eat whatever we could scrounge for.
And the next morning, they brought a wagon with two horses.
They gave us the wagon.
So the seven of us got on the wagon with two horses.
And we-- we ran on those horses until the horses dropped.

Finally, after several, several days of walking, and to going on the train a little bit, going with horses a little bit, and-- and the Czechs were-- I can't even describe to you how wonderful they were.
Well, my mother and myself.
My father went to Vienna from-- from there, to Raimund Titsch, to get the jewels that he gave him to hide.
It was my mother and myself, with Rabbi Lewertow.
It was a man by the-- by a name of [?
Hudis ?], whom I remember, and the other three, I can't-- I can't remember anymore.
As soon as we crossed the Polish-Czech border, in a town called [?
Swietochlowice ?], we were standing in a queue, Red Cross, a line for-- for soup.
The Poles in the line said, Hitler didn't do such a good job.
Look at the Jews who survived.
He didn't kill all of them.
Why don't you go to Palestine?
Why are you coming back to Poland?' Which we were used to, and we disregarded.
We got on the train and we went to Krakow.
We couldn't get our apartment back.
But we got, in the same building, a little apartment that belonged to somebody else.
In the meantime, my father came from Vienna, and he secured one of the factories manufacturing bicycle parts.
He got it back, but it was nationalized.
But he said, you can manage it.
So he put me as a-- as a manager.
And I was managing the factory for a few months.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day, I was going back from the factory, Saturday noontime, and a Polish woman with-- with a carriage and little baby, walking on the bridge, was looking at me and looking at me.
And she stopped.
And I stopped.
And I said, 'Why are you looking at me?' She says, 'Excuse me.
Please don't be offended.
Are you Jewish?' I said, 'Yes.'
'I would suggest that you don't go down that street, because they beating the Jewish people.' And I couldn't believe that this is after the war.
This is already August of 1945.
And Jews who survive are being beaten by the Poles.
So I started going.
And as I was getting closer to a certain point, I saw masses of people yelling, 'Kill the Jews!
Beat the Jews!' And this was right around a building that was, before the war, building for Jewish students who lived outside of Krakow, but were-- but were studying in Krakow.
And after the war, it was a building for Jewish refugees, housing Jewish refugees from the east.
Well, I started running home, because my parents were home.
And as soon as I came home, my father and my mother, and my father's friend who came for Saturday dinner-- dinnertime was noontime-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were there.
And they were pale, because they heard people running down the street. 'Kill the Jews!
Beat the Jews!'
So I walked over, and I closed the window.
About two minutes later, somebody's knocking on the door.
And four policemen and a civilian walk into the apartment and says, 'Who was shooting out of the window?' It was a pretext.
I said, 'We don't have a gun.
Nobody was shooting.'
'Search.' But they didn't really search.
He says, 'Take them out.' And they took us out on the street.
And right away, hundreds of Poles-- 'Kill the Jews.
Beat the Jews.' And I was  sure that they're going to lynch us.
At that moment, a detachment of Polish police was going to make order.
And an officer was on a horse in front.
And a schoolmate of mine became a high officer of police after the war.
So I started yelling that I am a friend of [?
Adam ?] [?
Kuchil ?].
Please do something for us.

And this officer came off the horse, and walk over, and says, 'What's going on?' I said, 'They took us out of there.' 'Why'd you do that?' They said, 'We were told by the civilian.' 'Who was the civilian?' They don't know.
He says, 'You watch them, that nothing happens to them.' He got back on the horse, and he went.
In the meantime, we decided that we are going to run for it, and first my father, then my mother, then my father's friend and I.
And we might in some friends' apartment, some friends who converted after the war to Catholics.
And we met in their apartment.
And we were afraid to go back to our apartment for two days.
And we stayed there two days.
After two days, we came back.
And right there and then, I decided that I'm leaving Poland.
I don't want any part of it.
And sure enough, in December of 1945, I made arrangements to leave with another couple of friends, a husband and a wife.
And we came to Polish city that was on the border of Czechoslovakia.
There were made arrangements that we could cross the border into Czechoslovakia.
We came to Czechoslovakia.
We came-- went to a house in [?
Czech ?] city of [?
Chechen. ?] We came to the house of the head of the Jewish community.
And it was a recommendation that he took us in.
And he had two-- two daughters.
And we were there two days, and the daughters sort of made a party, and they invited me.
And then a Czech officer walks in, and he was from my school, from Krakow.
And I asked him, 'How did you become a Czech officer?' So he-- he told me his story, how he escaped during the war.
He was with the Czech underground for three and a half years.
And he became a Czech officer.
I said, 'We want to go to the German border.
We want to go to Prague, but we don't have any papers.' He says, 'Don't worry.
Two days from now, there'll be a train going through [?
Chechen ?] to Prague.
I'll put you into the car with the foreign minister.
He's traveling.
Nobody will ask you for papers.
And he did it, and we came to Prague.
To make the story short, in Prague, he made arrangements with a taxi driver to take us to the city of As, which was on the Czech border.
And he told him, told the taxi driver, that we're Americans.
And he said to us, 'Don't you dare speak in Polish or German or anything-- only English.' Sure enough, we didn't talk much, because we couldn't converse in English.
When we came to As, it was evening already.
And the taxi driver walks into the finest hotel and tells them that we are Americans, and we're go to Germany.
At that time, this was a Communist country.
Americans, I figured that within 10, 15 minutes, the police would come and ask us for papers.
We checked into the room.
I took the elevator into the basement.
There was a trap door into the alley.
I opened the trap door.
I went back.
I told my friends, 'Let's go.' We took the valises and, through the trap door, we walked out.
And we started walking on the streets.
We don't know where we're going.
And it's getting dark already.
And while we're walking for about an hour on the streets, a little boy, about 15-year-old boy, walks by and says to us, [? 'Amhu?' ?]
[? 'Amhu' ?] was like a code word.
Am-- you know what 'am' is in Hebrew, is 'nation.' [? 'Amhu' ?] means 'from the nation.' And whenever one Jew met another Jew, ask, [? 'Amhu?' ?] Are you from the nation?
And I said, [? 'Amhu,' ?] and I turned around.
And he started speaking Yiddish to us.
And he says, 'We want to go to Germany.' He says, 'Come with me.' And he took us to a basement, where there were about 14 people sitting.
And there were two Jews from Palestine, from the Bricha, who were smuggling people over the border to bring them to Palestine.
And that's how we went over the border to Bavaria, in snow, New Year's Eve, 1945-1946.
And that little boy was our leader through the border.
When he'd show like this, we stop or we go.
And we came to Bavaria.
We came to a city called Selb.
And then I got on the train, and we went to Munich.
And from Munich, I went to a DP  camp-- displaced persons camp-- called Feldafing, where my uncle was.
And that's where I stayed after the war.
Then they opened the university in Munich.
And I made the exams.
And I entered the engineering school.
Besides that, I also got a job in camp, in the HIAS, in the Immigration Department.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And within a few months, I was in the Immigration Department.
I tried to immigrate myself and my parents.
My parents came, too.
And we got the visa, American visa, New Year's-- Erev Rosh Hashanah, 1946.
But there was a strike going on.
So they sent us to Bremen, but we couldn't leave for United States, because the ships weren't going.
So I spent days and evenings, just playing bridge, every single day, with some people.
And finally, we got on a ship, end of December.
It was-- the ship was called which arrived January 13 in New York Harbor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very sick on the ship.
On the fourth day, I got so sick that I was-- I thought I was dying.
And we got into a-- we called it an SOS, because we-- we landed in Halifax, Canada.
It was such a rough voyage.
And HIAS took us into their quarters.
They had an office downtown New York.
And that's where we slept-- in the HIAS office.
I had an uncle in Chicago who sent the papers for us.
And as I was leaving the pier, a man from Krakow was standing and waiting at the pier for somebody else who was on the-- on the ship.
And he was a young doctor from Krakow whom I knew well.

And I asked him, 'Sam, who is here among other people?' And he says, this one, that one. 'And Rena is here.' I said, 'You have her phone number?' He says, 'They don't have a phone, but I give you the phone number of her uncle.' And the first thing I came to HIAS, I called, and she came over.
And two days later, I left for Chicago.
We started corresponding.
The arrival in Chicago was really funny, because my uncle was one hell of a nice man, a terrific man.
But he had a son who was a sickly-- sickly one.
Was a couple of years older than I was.
They waited for us at the railroad station.
My cousin was driving a 12-cylinder Lincoln.
At that time, they made, in 1946, a 12-cylinder Lincoln with push-button doors.
It was the most expensive American car.
He was standing at the railroad station with a very long cigar.
And they brought us to their house.
And we had lunch.
And first, my cousin was showing me a banana.
He says, 'This is called banana.' And-- and he was showing us all kinds of things, and I thought that this was so ridiculous.
I said to my father, 'You know something?
I can't take it.
Who does he think we are?
Where does he think we came from?' He says, 'Be nice.
Be nice.' Then my cousin takes us into the alley.
He says, 'You see this car?
This is the best American car.' He says, 'This is called automobile.'
At that moment, his wife calls him to the telephone.
And I get into the car, I start it, and I drive around the block.
And in Chicago, they parked the cars in the alley in the back.
When I came back into the alley, the whole family was standing.
My cousin was pale.
He says, 'How did you know how to drive?' I said, 'Benny, I have an international driver's license.
Don't worry about it.' So he got on the telephone.

He called all his friends. 'My greenhorn knows how to drive.' So I said to them, 'Look, I want to finish engineering.' So my cousin tells  me, says to me, 'In this country, you don't need education to make a success.' He says, 'Look at me.' He says, 'If you work for us long enough, you'll make, one day, $100 a week.'
Well, I came to New York on a Saturday, and Monday I was already at work, working for my-- my uncle, actually, but my cousin was the big boss.
They put me into their warehouse.
They had plumbing and heating supplies.
And they had six stores and a huge warehouse.
And they put me into warehouse.
And I work loading and unloading railroad cars when the-- when the bathtubs were coming, and the-- and the pipes, and the-- and the hot water heaters, loading and unloading.
I wasn't very happy with this-- with this job.
And then they transferred me-- deliveries to people's home.
Delivering bathtubs to the fourth or fifth floor, schlepping it on a-- on a staircase.
And I quit.
I got into an argument with my cousin.
Oh, they prepared the-- my uncle prepared an apartment for us above one of his stores.
He owned all the buildings where his stores were.
Which was a sort of a shopping street, busy, but look, apartment with two or three bedrooms was terrific.
We were very thankful.
My father, in the meantime, decided that he will try to get back into goldsmithing.
So he set up a little bench, and he started working in the house.
And he worked for five, six months.
And he said, I'm ready to do some work.
So after work, I used to go, in the evening to jewelry stores, and get repairs, and bring them in.
This eventually developed-- like, before Christmas we were getting so much work that I learned how to file the rings.
My father was soldering and my mother was polishing.
And we worked 20 hours a day, day in and day out.
And one day, I decided that I'm going to quit working, and I'm going to get more into this-- this line.
And I acquired a little office in Chicago, in somebody's office, a room.
I set up a bench there for my father.
And I started going around stores, getting repairs.
And from that, it developed into a little business where-- oh, first I went to New York.
I heard that somebody's looking for a salesman in Chicago, selling ring mountings.
And I went to New York, and he gave me his line, and I brought it back.
But I had difficulty selling it, because the prices were high.
So I found out that he's really not manufacturing those rings, that it's somebody else who's casting and making those rings.
I found out his source.
And I came back to New York, and I went to the source, and I said to the man, 'Look, I don't have much money.
But if you give me--' and I told him my life story.
'If you give me a chance, give me like $3,000 credit, I assure you, you won't lose a penny. 'I will sell.' He says, 'I'll think about it.
Come with me to lunch.' And he took me to lunch.
And we came back, he says, 'Pick what you want to pick.' And I figured that I will not pick $3,000.
I'll pick about $2,000.
And he gave-- gave the credit to me.
And I came back to Chicago.
I sold everything.
I made a little profit.
I came back to New York.
I said, 'Now I want $4,000 credit.' And this developed into [? certain ?] business.
But when we got engaged, Rena and myself, Rena was not too willing to live in Chicago, to leave her mother and father here.
So I decided to move to New York.
And we moved to New York, and my father and mother, and we had a very hard time to establish ourselves in New York.
But eventually, we did.
And I started going out and selling.
And then we got an idea to print little catalogues.
And this caught on, and then we established an office.
And in the beginning of 1960s, I found out about Italian jewelry manufacturers.
And I started going to Italy, and acquiring some of the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not completed jewelry, but what they called the mountings.
I started buying mountings and bringing them to the United States and setting them with diamonds and-- and other stones.
And I started selling.
And this thing went very well.
And then I started going to Paris, getting-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what do they call it?
Getting designs.
Bring them to Italy, manufacture in Italy.
In the meantime, after we got married in 1949, in June 19th, we had a little apartment in Washington Heights.
And in 1951, our first son Arthur was born.
So we moved to a little bit bigger apartment.
Well, we had little mice running around.
It was on the main floor.
But-- but it was great for us.
We already started going skiing.
I bought my first car in around 1951, which was a secondhand car.
And we started going places.
In summer, we rented a little house on the lake with our parents for $300 for the whole summer, when Arthur was born.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1954, our second son was born, Lester.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we saw that we don't have enough room.
When we got our first money for German restitution, we used the money to buy a house.
And we bought a house in Long Island, in a place called Woodmere, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which seemed like a paradise to us.
Our parents lived in Washington Heights, in an area which was in Manhattan-- not the greatest area, but with a lot of Jews from Germany and refugees living there.
And they were visiting us every weekend.
And we made two rooms for them where they could sleep and-- and live with us.
Arthur started going to elementary school in Woodmere.
He was an excellent student.
And he right away showed his musical abilities.
At first, in New York, when he was about five years old, he started taking accordion lessons, because we couldn't afford a piano.
But once we were in Woodmere and my business got a little better, we bought a secondhand piano.
And he was taking lessons, and he was continuously practicing.
But continuuosly-- it went so far that I used to tell him, 'Please stop, already.
I can't take it.' And the boys went to local schools.
And eventually, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I sent them to a private school, private high school, which was Woodmere Academy.
And they both were excellent students.
Excellent.
And we lived in Woodmere until 1976, when we bought a much bigger and better house in a place called Hewlett Harbor.
By that time, Arthur was accepted to many schools, and among them, he picked Connecticut Wesleyan, because they had a very good music department.
By that time, he was already taking conducting lessons, also, from Laszlo Halasz, who was the head of the City Center Opera in New York.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was a great pianist already by that time.
And within a couple of years, Lester was accepted to Amherst College.
And a terrific student.
And he decided at one point that he wants to become a rabbi.
And he applied for divinity scholarship from Amherst.
And this was the first case at Amherst College, first case, that they had a Jewish student going to rabbinical school.
You know, of Amherst College, which is very WASP-ish.
And in the meantime, Arthur was also accepted-- while he was at Wesleyan, he was accepted to Curtis Institute of Music.
And he graduated from Curtis.
And Christoph von Dohnanyi, who is today the music director of Cleveland Orchestra-- Cleveland Symphony Orchestra-- was a German, a German conductor-- he--
